California
Fair Political
Practices Cornrnission

ol,son, conne11y, Hage1, Fong and Leidigh
3oO capitol Mal1, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA 95814
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-38 6
oear l,ts . Fishburn:
You have requested advice on behalf of your client ce11a Barr
Associates (ncBAr') and the city of Riverbank (the itcityr') regard-
ing their obligations under the confl ict-of- interest provisions of
th; Political 
Ref orrn Act (the rrasg't 1 .1
OUESTIONS
cBA is the successful bidder on a proposed contract (the
rrcontractrr) with the city. 
The contract provides for the
preparation of studies and plans for the adoption of a specific
pfan fy the city of a 6oo-acre unincorporated area adjacent to the
city limits (the "planning arearr) .
1. 
would cBA staff be ttpublic off icialstt within the meaning
of Government code sections 871-oo and 87103 lrhen providing
services pursuant to the contract?
2. 
will the cBA staff's participation j-n the city's an-
nexation and planning of the planning area create a conflict of
interest?
CONCLUSTON
1. 
The cBA staff (as defined below) are public officials
within the meaning of Government code sections 87100 and 87103'
1 Go.r".r,r"nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. Al-L statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commi-ssion
regulations appear at 2 California Code of ReguLations . Section
18600, et seq-. AlL references to regulations are to Title 2,
Dj-vision 5 of the California code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 i Sacramento CA 958O4.O8O7 . (916)322'5660

Page 2
A-90-386
2. 
Tfre cBA's staff's partlcipation in the city's annexation
and planning of the planning area will create a conflict of
interest if as a result of their participation the gross revenues
of cBA wiLl- increase or decrease $10,000 in a fiscal year.
FACTS
1. 
Entities and Individuals
The facts concerning the varj-ous entities and individuals
j-nvotved in this request for advice are as folIo$rs:
A.
CBA
cBA is a private consulting f irrn. It provides planning and
consulting servj.ces in engineering, surveying, landscape
qrchitecture, and hydrology to public and private entities. 
cBA
is a closely held corPoration.
B. 
The cBA Staff
Don LaidLaw is a vice-president of cBA and owns approximately
3.4 percent of the total outstanding shares. He wilL be the
project manager of the contract. other cBA staff lri11 be
j-nvo1ved, as wel-1 as several subcontractors seLected by cBA. None
of these individuals owns a ten percent or greater interest in
cBA, nor have any of these individuals provided services directly
to Lloyd Glasgow. (Mr. Laidlaw and the other cBA staff working on
the contract will be collectively referred to as the rrcBA staff -rr)
c. 
cBA's Relationship with Llovd Glasgow
Mr. Glasgow is a land developer who has done business both as
a sole owner (Liberty Developments ) and as a partner in a business
entity (Gruen winters) in which he has a one-third interest- 
Mr-
Glasgow and cBA have had an on-going business relationship for
several years.
Mr. Glasgow owns or has an interest in approxinately 120 to
150 acres in the 6oo-acre planning area. None of this area is
zoned, and the land is currentl.y undeveloped. If the specific
plan is adopted by the city, Mr. Glasgow may proceed with develop-
ment of his property in the planning area.
2. 
Contract with Citv of Riverbank
The contract incorporates the terms of CBA,s proposal (the
'rproposal't) to the city. 
(See Contract.z) Under the contract,
CBA $/iLl conduct some of the prelininary studies and oversee the
preparation of other studies by subcontractors. CBA vrif L also
The contract and proposal were submitted with your letter.


our File No.
Page 3
A-90-386
develop the draft specific plan itself and the draft environmental
irupact report.
The contract generally provides that cBA and the other
members of the consultant team will closely consult and coordinate
with the city's staff and other public agencies.
ANALYSIS
1. 
PubIic official
section 87100 prohibits any public official 
frorn making, or
otherwise using his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official 
has a financial interest. 
A
rrpublic official" 
is defined in Section 82048 to include every
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency. Regulation 18700 (copy encl-osed) defines
rrconsuLtant!t:
rrconsultantrr shaII include any natural person vrho Provides
under contract, inforrnation, advice recolnmendat j-on or counsel
to a state or 1ocal government agency, provj.ded, however,
that trconsultant shall not include a person who:
(A) conducts research and arrives at conclusions with
respect to his or her rendition of information, advice,
recommendation, or counsel independent of control and
direction of the agency or any agency official, 
other
than normal contract nonitoring; and
(B) Possesses no authorj,ty with respect to any agency
decision beyond the rendition of information, advice,
recomrnendation or counsel .
Regulation 18700 (a) (2).
The commission explained 18700(a) (2) in In re ltaloney (1977)
3 FPPC Ops. 69 (copy enclosed) , concLuding that a certain survey-
engineer was not a consultant.
when perforrning these services, the county surveyor-engineer
is not involved in any official decision naking. He is
merely carrying out the terms of decision naking. He is
nerely carryingT out the terns of contract just as any vendor
of goods or services to the county night. 
Ile is not subiect
to the control or discretion of the countv when he performs
his work, but is ooverned onlv bv the provisions of his
contract .
Id. at 71 (enphasis added) .
The cBA staff are consultants. It is the nature of this
contract that the CBA staff provide information, advice, recom-
mendation or counsel. to the city. 
Under the contract, cBA wj,f 1


our -b.l--Le No.
Page 4
A-90-386
provide extensive amounts of j,nformation as a result of research
and numerous studies involving traffic, 
wastewater treatment, sew-
ers, drainage, $rater servj.ce, city buildings, city needs for
capital equiprnent, financing, the housing market and other issues.
cBA will provide recomnendations and advice to the city on these
issues. In particular, 
cBA will provide a draft specific plan for
development of the planning area for the city's adoption.
The contract provides for a significant amount of control and
direction by the city. 
At ninirnum, it is evident from the cBA
proposal that such control and direction will- occur during the
start-up period (Proposal, p. 2), in the traffic 
study (ProPosal-,
p. 3), in the infrastructure study (Proposal, p. 5 rrin close co-
ordination with the City Engineer, the city Manager and staff rr),
in creation of the Park Master PIan (ProPosal, p. 10), in the
f j.nancial study (Proposal, pp. 13, 15), j-n the physical planning
(Broposal, p. 21 tra closely coordinated, coll-aborating planning
effort between cBA, tean members, the City Manager and staff, the
city Engineer and the Planning comrnissiontr), in the creation of
plan alternatives (Proposaf, pp. 23, 24 itwith the concurrence by
ttre city Manager, cBA will prepare a Preliutinary Draft Specific
planrt), and in the creation of the specific pfan (Proposal, p. 24
i'cBA and its team . . trill coordi,nate extensi.vely, (internally
and with city and agency staff and comrnissions) ," p. 29), ft is
clear that a considerable amount of opportunities exist for the
city's control and direction.
Thus $/e conclude that the CBA staff are consultants and
therefore 'rpublic offi.cials" within the purvie$, of the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Act.
2. 
Conflict of f ntelcs'L
The Act provides a four-part test to determine whether a
pubtic official 
has a conflict of interest in a particular
governmental decision. First, is the official- rnaking, participat-
ing in making, or using his official 
position to influence a
governmental, decision? (Section 87100.) Second, is it
foreseeable that the decision !'/i11 affect the official's 
economic
interest? 
(Section 87103.) Third, will the effect of the
decj.sion on the official's 
econoruic interest be material? (Id.)
Fourth, is the effect of the decision on the official's 
economic
interest distinguishable from its effect on the public generally?
(Id. 87103.) (See qenerallv, West Advj.ce Letter, No. A-88-406,
copy enclosed. )
A. 
Participation fn A Governmental Decision
Regnrlation 18700 defines making or participating in making a
governmental decision. 1870o(c) (2) provides that a public of-
ficial participates in a governnental decision when he advises or
makes recommendations to a decision naker, without significant
intervening substantive review, by (A) conducting resiarch or nak-
ing any investigation which reguires the exercise of j udgment on

A-90-386
the part of the offic■
a■ and the purpose of which is to inf■
uence
the decision′  or (B)preparing or presenting any report′
 ana■ysis
or opinion which requires the exercise of judgment and the purpose
of which is to influence the decision.
The CBA staff wi■
■ be participating in a governmenta■
 deci―
sion.  The CBA staff will prepare and promote the adoption of
various technica■
 and policy documents for the city′
s annexation
and deve■opment of the p■anning areao  Such documents′
 in their
various recommendations, wi■■ constitute the exercise of judgment
on the part of the CBA staff and the documents wi■
■ eventually
influence the city′
s decisions on annexation and planning.  In
particu■ar′  the CBA staff wi■
■ be drafting (and in effect recom―
mending)a specific plan that wi■
■ dete.1lline zoning and the 
■oca―
tion of pub■ic faci■ities′  open space and streets. (See contract
and Proposal′  pp. 26-27.) The annexation of the p■
anning area and
the adoption of a specific p■an are certain■y governmenta■ deci―
s■ons, and CBA certa■nly participlt民

governmenta■ decisiOns.  (See Wes
B.
Foreseeable Financ■
a■ Effect
The second issue is the foreseeability that the decision wil-I
affect the official's 
economic interest. 
The parameters of
public officlal's 
economic interest are set forth by Section
87103. r'or the purposes of the question at hand,
An official, has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeabl,e that the deci.sion will have
a rnaterial financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the pubJ.ic generally, on the offici.al
or a member of his or he j-nmediate fanily or on:
,", 
'o"" 
source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans 
aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or nore in vafue
provided to, received by or promised to the
3 Yo.r. letter does not present any discussion or facts indicating
that the city will conduct a significant intervening substantive
revj.ew of cBA's participation. 
"Signifi-cant intervening
substantive review'r is interpreted narrowly. Even if a
consultant,s participation in a decj-sion is rrreviewed" by the
governmental entity, if the entity relies on the data or analysis
prepared by the consultant $rithout checking it independently, if
it relies on the professional judgment of the consu.Ltant, or if
the consultant in sone other $/ay actual-ly may influence the finaL
decision, there is no signj.ficant intervening substantive review.

public official 
within
time when the decision

nonths prior to the
made.
Section 87103.
The cBA staff are employed by cBA and presumably r,riIl have
received $250 or more in income for their services from cBA in the
12 months prior to any decj-sion. Therefore, we must examine the
foreseeabli fj-nancial- effect on cBA, the ttsource of income. "4
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantiaL likefihood that it will- occur. Something more
than a mere possibility that the effect will occur must exist.
(In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198, copy enclosed.) Given the
facts you have presented, there is a substantial likeli.hood that
the city's decision on the annexation and ptanning of the planningt
area $rouId have a financial effect on cBA. Mr. Glasgow is a
d6veloper. He o$rns or has an interest in as rnuch as 25 percent of
the Ia;d in the planning area. Mr. Glasgow plans to develop his
property in the planning area. As we discussed in our telephone
Lonversltion of June 20, 1990, if Mr. Glasgo\^/ cannot develop his
property, he may se1l it.
As stated in your letter, since Mr. Glasgow is an established
cl j.ent of CBA, it is Iikely that CBA will be retained by Mr-
clasgo$, to provide services in connection with the develoPment of
his property in the planning area. It is reasonably foreseeable
that Mr. Glisgtow will continue to retain cBA for services in the
future, and at the very least cBA has a reasonable expectation
that Mr. Glasgow wi1l, continue to retain cBA. As we discussed in
our telephone conversation of June 20, 1990, while rnany decisions
regarding the specific plan have already been rnade by the city,
there remains much room for the cBA staff's 
and the city's
discretion. Thus vrhether Mr. GLasgol.r's land is zoned conmercial,
residenti.al, mixed or open space, srhether there are roads on or
near his land, whether there is a park or school on or near his
land and li.ke questions will deterrnine what developnent Mr.
Glasgow wilL do and affect how nuch business he wifl send to CBA.
fn sum, there appears a substantiaL Likelihood that CBA'S
4 B"".t,." none of the cBA staff has a 10 percent or greater
ownership interest in CBA, sources of income to CBA, in this case
Mr, Glasgow, will not be considered sources of income to the CBA
staff. 
(Sectj.on 82030,. Moe Advice Letter, A-89-454, copy
enclosed. )


our File No. A-9 0-3 8 6
Page 7
recommendations to the city wiII have an effect on how nuctr
business it receives from Mr. Glasgow.5
c. 
Materialitv
In order to deterrnine if a decision's effect is material, it
must first 
be determined $rhether the pubtic official's 
economic
interest is directly involved j,n the decision and whetfrer tfre
effect of the decision j-s rnaterial under Section 18702.1 (copy
enclosed) . If the official's 
economic interest is not directly
involved in the decision, or the effect of the decision is not
materiaf under Section 18702.1,, then it rnust be determined whether
the decision indirectly affects the official's 
economic interest'
Materiality is then determined under the appropriate regulation in
Sections L8702.2 through 78702.6 (copy enclosed). (Regulation
1A7O2, copy enclosed. )
The CBA staff's participation j.n the annexation and planning
of the planning area does not appear to have a direct effect upon
the cBA staff's 
economj.c interests under the facts provided to us.
As none of the CBA staff has an ownership interest in cBA greater
than ten percent, any benefit to Mr. Glasgow does not have any
direct effect upon the cBA staff. 
(Regulation 7a7o2.1; see also
Moe Advice Letter, suDra).
The CBA staff's economic interests may, however, be
indirectl-y involved. Under this analysis, whether the effect of
the transiction is naterj.al is dependent on the size of the busi-
ness entity. 
In our telephone conversation of June 20, 1990, you
informed me that cBA would fa1l within the asset and incorne
standards under Regulation 18702.2(S). Given such figures, the
participation of the cBA staff in the decisions of the city are
material if as to cBA:
(1) The decision wil,1 result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or
5_
- In your letter of Uay 29 | ]-99O, and as clarified in our
telephone conversation of June 20, t99O, you argued that v/hether
or not the specific plan proposed by CBA is adopted will have no
effect on CBA,S relationship with Mr. Glasgow. you said that if
the planning area is not annexed, Mr. clasgow nay ve1I se11 his
property in the planning area and choose to develop elsewhere,
giving CBA the same anount of work anyeray. However, absent any
set expectation or agreement that Mr. clasgol,, will give CBA the
same anount of work, it is difficult 
to rely on your assertion.
ReLiance is especially difficult 
when one compares the some\^rhat
uncertai.n expectation that Mr. clasgow tiII 
buy and develop other
property lrith the certainty that Mr. ctasgow rrilt develop his
property in the planning area if it is annexed and ptann-d by the
c ity.


Our Fi■e No.  A-90-386
Page 8
(2) The decision r.riL]. result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating exj-sting expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,5oo or more,' or
(3) The decj-sion will result in an increase
or decrease in the val-ue of assets or liabilities
of $10, OO0 or more.
Regulation le7 02 .2 (g) .
The question of noment appears to be lrhether the annexation
and planning of the planning area will- result in an increase or
decrlase of business to cBA from Mr' Glasgo\" of $10'000 0r more in
a fiscal year. 
Given cBA's past business history with Mr.
Gl-asgow a;d the known amount of Land Mr. Glasgord has an interest
in the planning area' this figure should be subject to reasonable
calculation.
D. 
Public Generallv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is rnaterial, disqualif ication is required only if the
effect is distinguishable for the public generall-y. (section
87103.) If the decision does not affect all the members of the
public in the same manner, disqualification may be required unless
Lhe effect of the decision on the source of income is the same as
the effect on a significant segnnent of the public. 
(Regulation
18703, copy enclosed. )
The decisions to be made in connection $rith the development
of the planning area wil-l neither affect all the menbers of the
public in the same manner nor will the effect be the same on a
significant seqment of the public. Ilere an increase or decrease
of business from Mr. G]asgolr, wilt affect cBA uniquely'
In sum, given the facts presented cBA it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decisions regarding the annexation and
development of the planning area, j-n which cBA is planning to
participate in, will have a financial effect on CBA, and such
effect is distinguishable from the public aenerally. If this
financial effect is rnaterial as set forth in Regulation
lA7O2.2(g), then there will be a conflict of j-nterest.
